perturbation.original_text: (4) Without the prior written consent of the other party, each party will not, and will cause its Representatives not to, disclose to any person either the status of the investigations, discussions or negotiations taking place concerning a possible transaction between the parties, or that either party has requested or received Evaluation Material from the other party or any of the terms, conditions or other facts with respect to any such possible transaction except as disclosed in that certain letter of intent between the parties dated February 25, 2002. The term "person" as used in this Agreement will be interpreted broadly to include, without limitation, any corporation, company, partnership or individual.
perturbation.changed_text: (4) Without the prior written consent of the other party, each party will not, and will cause its Representatives not to, disclose to any person either the status of the investigations, discussions or negotiations taking place concerning a possible transaction between the parties, or that either party has requested or received Evaluation Material from the other party or any of the terms, conditions or other facts with respect to any such possible transaction except as disclosed in that certain letter of intent between the parties dated February 25, 2002. The term "person" as used in this Agreement will be interpreted to only include natural persons.
perturbation.explanation: The definition of "person" is restricted to only include natural persons, which contradicts the broad legal definition often used in corporate law that includes corporations and other entities.
perturbation.law_explanation: This section states that 'person' includes corporations, companies, associations, firms, partnerships, societies, and joint stock companies, as well as individuals. The modification limits the definition to 'natural persons,' creating a direct contradiction.

(1/4/1995) Back to Original Document << Previous TITLE 1 / CHAPTER 1 / § 1 Next >> [Print] [Print selection] [OLRC Home] Help 1 USC 1 : Words denoting number, gender, and so forth Text contains those laws in effect on June 22, 2025 From Title 1-GENERAL PROVISIONS CHAPTER 1-RULES OF CONSTRUCTION Jump To: Source Credit Miscellaneous Amendments Short Title §1. Words denoting number, gender, and so forth In determining the meaning of any Act of Congress, unless the context indicates otherwise- words importing the singular include and apply to several persons, parties, or things; words importing the plural include the singular; words importing the masculine gender include the feminine as well; words used in the present tense include the future as well as the present; the words "insane" and "insane person
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 1 U.S. Code § 1 - Words denoting number, gender, and so forth | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 1 U.S. Code § 1 - Words denoting number, gender, and so forth U.S. Code Notes prev | next In determining the meaning of any Act of Congress , unless the context indicates otherwise— words importing the singular include and apply to several persons , parties, or things; words importing the plural include the singular; words importing the masculine gender include the feminine as well; words used in the present tense include the future as well as the present; the words “ insane ” and “ insane person ” shall include every idiot, insane person , and person non compos mentis; the words “ person ” and “whoever” include corporations, companies, associations, firms, partnerships, societies, and joint stock companies, as well as individuals ; “ officer ” includes any person authorized by law to perform the duties of the office; “ signature ” or “ subscription ” includes a mark when the person making the same intended it as such; “ oath ” includes affirmation, and “ sworn ” includes affirmed; “ writing ” includes printing and typewriting and reproductions of visual symbols by photographing, multigraphing, mimeographing, manifolding, or otherwise. (July 30, 1947, ch. 388, 61 Stat. 633 ; June 25, 1948, ch. 645, § 6, 62 Stat. 859 ; Oct. 31, 1951, ch. 655, § 1, 65 Stat. 710 ; Pub. L. 112–231, § 2(a) , Dec. 28, 2012 , 126 Stat. 1619 .) Editorial Notes Amendments 2012— Pub. L. 112–231 , in fifth clause after opening clause, struck out “and ‘lunatic’ ” before “shall include every” and “lunatic,” before “insane person, ”. 1951—Act Oct. 31, 1951 , substituted, in fourth clause after opening clause, “used” for “use”. 1948—Act June 25, 1948 , included “tense”, “whoever”, “signature” , “subscription” , “writing” and a broader definition of “person” . Statutory Notes and Related Subs
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the standard legal definition of 'person'. The original text explicitly states the term 'person' will be interpreted broadly, including corporations, companies, partnerships, or individuals. The changed text limits the definition of 'person' to only include natural persons. Scraped Law Snippet 2, specifically 1 U.S. Code § 1, states that 'person' and 'whoever' include corporations, companies, associations, firms, partnerships, societies, and joint stock companies, as well as individuals. This direct conflict with the legally recognized definition constitutes a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: (6) Each party understands and acknowledges that the other party is making no representation or warranty, express or implied, as to the accuracy or completeness of the Evaluation Material, and neither the disclosing party, nor any of its respective officers, directors, employees, stockholders, affiliates or agents will have any liability to the other party or any other person resulting from such other party's use of the Evaluation Material.
perturbation.changed_text: (6) Each party understands and acknowledges that the other party is making no representation or warranty, express or implied, as to the accuracy or completeness of the Evaluation Material, and neither the disclosing party, nor any of its respective officers, directors, employees, stockholders, affiliates or agents will have any liability to the other party or any other person resulting from such other party's use of the Evaluation Material, even in cases of gross negligence or willful misconduct.
perturbation.explanation: By explicitly excluding liability even for gross negligence or willful misconduct, the contract attempts to waive liabilities that cannot be waived under Delaware law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware law on exculpation of directors
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware law allows corporations to include provisions in their certificate of incorporation to limit or eliminate personal liability of directors for monetary damages for breach of fiduciary duty. However, this exculpation does not extend to acts or omissions not in good faith or which involve intentional misconduct or a knowing violation of law. The added clause attempts to eliminate liability even in such cases.

General Corporation Law Subchapter II. Powers Â§ 121. General powers. (a) In addition to the powers enumerated in Â§â122 of this title, every corporation, its officers, directors and stockholders shall possess and may exercise all the powers and privileges granted by this chapter or by any other law or by its certificate of incorporation, together with any powers incidental thereto, so far as such powers and privileges are necessary or convenient to the conduct, promotion or attainment of the business or purposes set forth in its certificate of incorporation. (b) Every corporation shall be governed by the provisions and be subject to the restrictions and liabilities contained in this chapter. 8 Del. C. 1953, Â§ â121;Â 56 Del. Laws, c. 50. ;Â Â§ 122. Specific powers [For application of this section, see 84 Del. Laws, c. 309, Â§ 6]. Every corporation created under this chapter shall have power, whether or not so provided in the certificate of incorporation, to: (1) Have perpetual succession by its corporate name, unless a limited period of duration is stated in its certificate of incorporation; (2) Sue and be sued in all courts and participate, as a party or otherwise, in any judicial, administrative, arbitrative or other proceeding, in its corporate name; (3) Have a corporate seal, which may be altered at pleasure, and use the same by causing it or a facsimile thereof, to be impressed or affixed or in any other manner reproduced; (4) Purchase, receive, take by grant, gift, devise, bequest or otherwise, lease, or otherwise acquire, own, hold, improve, employ, use and otherwise deal in and with real or personal property, or any interest therein, wherever situated, and to sell, convey, lease, exchange, transfer or otherwise dispose of, or mortgage or pledge, all or any of its property and assets, or any interest therein, wherever situated; (5) Appoint such
perturbation.justification: The modified text attempts to eliminate liability even in cases of gross negligence or willful misconduct. The Law Explanation states that Delaware law does not allow corporations to eliminate liability for acts or omissions not in good faith or which involve intentional misconduct or a knowing violation of law. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 8 Del. C. 1953, Â§ â121, outlines the general powers of a corporation, its officers, directors, and stockholders but it is not relevant to the contradiction at hand. The modified contract text directly contradicts the limitations of liability under Delaware law as explained, making the contradiction direct and serious.

perturbation.original_text: (8) Each party agrees that the other party shall be entitled to equitable relief, including injunction and specific performance, in the event of any breach of the provisions of this Agreement, in addition to all other remedies available to the other part at law or in equity.
perturbation.changed_text: (8) Each party agrees that the other party shall be entitled to monetary damages only in the event of any breach of the provisions of this Agreement, explicitly waiving any right to equitable relief, including injunction and specific performance, even if monetary damages are insufficient.
perturbation.explanation: This removes the option of equitable relief, which may be the only suitable recourse for breaches related to confidential information. Specific performance is often crucial in contracts where unique assets or actions are involved, and waiving it contradicts standard legal principles.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Principles of Equity under Delaware Law
perturbation.law_explanation: Under Delaware law, equitable remedies like specific performance and injunctions are available when monetary damages are inadequate. Waiving these remedies entirely, even when damages are insufficient, contradicts the court's inherent power to grant equitable relief in appropriate circumstances. The court can use its powers to enforce specific performance in cases it deems are necessary.

perturbation.justification: The original contract allows for equitable relief, including injunction and specific performance, in the event of a breach. The changed text explicitly waives any right to equitable relief, even if monetary damages are insufficient. The Law Explanation states that under Delaware law, equitable remedies are available when monetary damages are inadequate, and that waiving these contradicts the court's power to grant equitable relief. While the scraped snippets are not particularly useful, the Law Explanation and the Perturbation Explanation confirm that the modification introduces a direct contradiction by removing the availability of equitable relief, when it is legally permissible.

perturbation.original_text: (9) It is further understood and agreed that no failure or delay by either party in exercising any right, power or privilege hereunder will operate as a waiver thereof, nor will any single or partial exercise thereof preclude any other or further exercise thereof or the exercise of any right, power or privilege hereunder. This Agreement is for the benefit of the parties and will be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware.
perturbation.changed_text: (9) It is further understood and agreed that any failure or delay by either party in exercising any right, power or privilege hereunder will operate as a waiver thereof if such failure or delay persists for more than 30 days. This Agreement is for the benefit of the parties and will be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware.
perturbation.explanation: The modification introduces a strict 30-day time limit, potentially creating a scenario where a party unintentionally waives rights due to a short-term delay, contradicting the common legal principle of non-waiver for isolated delays.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General principles of contract law regarding waiver
perturbation.law_explanation: Under general contract law, a waiver requires intentional relinquishment of a known right. The Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 246 outlines performance acceptance as waiver. Introducing a 30-day automatic waiver clause could be seen as an unenforceable penalty or forfeiture if the delay is not indicative of a true intent to waive a right. Some delays can be explained and shouldn't automatically constitute a waiver.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: waiver | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you waiver A waiver is the intentional or voluntary relinquishment or surrender of a recognized right or privilege . In legal contexts, a waiver is often documented in writing, but it can also be implied through actions . By waiving a right, an individual or party chooses not to enforce a particular right, claim , or provision they are legally entitled to. [Last reviewed in July of 2024 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex accidents and injuries COMMERCE commercial activities LIFE EVENTS accidents & injuries (tort law) THE LEGAL PROCESS courts criminal procedure business law contracts wex definitions business sectors civil procedure individual rights legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a 30-day limit for waiving rights due to failure or delay. This directly contradicts the principle of waiver as 'the intentional or voluntary relinquishment or surrender of a recognized right or privilege' as defined in Scraped Law Snippet 2. The original text ensured that delays did not automatically constitute a waiver. By imposing a 30-day limit, the changed text creates a scenario where a waiver could be unintentionally triggered, undermining the requirement of intentionality. This aligns with the 'Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction' definition, as the contractual language deviates from the legally recognized definition of waiver.
